text: The surgeon performs robotic sigmoid dissection in a case of diverticulitis with a colovascular fistula. Adhesiolysis is conducted to free the small bowel from the anterior abdominal wall and pelvis, likely due to a past abscess. The small bowel is moved to the upper abdomen and packed with gauze. The surgeon begins a lateral to medial dissection, immobilizing tissue over the left kidney towards the splenic flexure.

The surgeon takes down omental adhesions in the left upper quadrant near the splenic flexure, then proceeds with careful dissection towards the phlegmon and bladder adherence, staying close to the colon to avoid the ureter. The suction device is used as a retractor and blunt dissector, clearing blood and smoke, and aiding in the approach to the colovascular fistula, which is visually identified as a hole in the bowel.

The surgeon exposes the gonadal vessels and inadvertently dissects into the colon wall, pulling adhesions from the fused retroperitoneum. The deep pelvis is identified, and the surgeon separates adhesions from the left pelvic brim. The dissection continues on the right side, addressing adhesions to the right adnexa, with visible inflammatory reactions in the deep pelvis.

The surgeon enlarges the right lower quadrant 12mm port site for specimen extraction, avoiding transrectal extraction due to potential rectal tearing from inflammation. The ureter is visible during this process. The surgeon seeks the distal margin while freeing inflammatory adhesions in the deep pelvis, utilizing suction for retraction and clearing the surgical field.

The surgeon is not using an assist port while taking down adhesions from the old vaginal cuff. The entire colon is lifted out of the pelvis, and the surgeon assesses the planned proximal margin, choosing its location. A window is created in the mesentery without performing a high ligation of the superior hemorrhoidal or the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA), opting to hug the bowel instead. The colon appears large and heavy with significant fat, making exposure challenging.

The surgeon works down to the upper rectum to identify the distal margin, discovering an unexpected loop of the sigmoid colon extending into the pelvis. With tension applied, the loop is lifted out of the pelvis, allowing the surgeon to clear the planned distal margin while checking the ureter and staying close to the mesentery.

The surgeon uses a stapler to divide the bowel proximally and distally, preparing for a sewn anastomosis. The distal margin is cleared, and the surgeon checks the length and perfusion before firing the stapler. The specimen is placed into the right abdomen, and a two-layer anastomosis is performed, joining the two staple lines as the posterior row.

The surgeon joins the posterior lip of the anastomosis and sews towards the left side, then starts in the posterior midline and sews towards the right side. The procedure involves a two-layer anastomosis, with sutures tied together and sewn out and around anteriorly, creating a secure connection between the bowel segments.

The surgeon completes the anastomosis and performs a leak test by irrigating the pelvis. The scope is inserted through the anastomosis, confirming no leakage. The surgical field is cleaned, and a drain is placed to remain for three days, while the Foley catheter stays for two days.
